caseStudy.title: How Adomantra Helped Brands Capture ₹234 Crores in Quick Commerce <r>Revenue</r>?
caseStudy.summary: While India's quick commerce market exploded to $6.94 billion in 2026 (projected to be $11.08 billion by 2030), most FMCG and D2C brands couldn't capitalize. They faced zero visibility on India's leading quick commerce platforms, marketplace algorithms penalizing new sellers, inability to optimize product listings, lack of performance marketing expertise, etc. Adomantra's quick commerce growth framework combines marketplace optimization, performance marketing, and omnichannel strategy. As a result, brands launched or scaled on quick commerce platforms and ended up generating ₹234 crores in combined quick commerce revenue, 340% average sales increase in Year 1, ₹89 crores incremental quick commerce revenue, 12-month payback period, and 4.2x average ROI on performance marketing spend.
caseStudy.projectobjective:
The Project

A 360-degree Quick Commerce Operating System deployed by Adomantra across three major CPG and FMCG brands (10on10 Foods, Kaya, and Patanjali).

The Objective

To establish quick commerce presence, optimize listings, deploy pincode-wise hyperlocal performance marketing, and coordinate cross-channel inventory to capture rapid delivery demand.

The Ultimate Result

₹234 Crores generated in combined quick commerce revenue, a 340% average sales increase in Year 1, and 4.2x average ROI on performance marketing spend.
caseStudy.leadParagraph:
THE CORE CHALLENGE: WHY WERE BRANDS LOSING MONEY?

By early 2026, India's quick commerce market had become impossible to ignore. Yet paradoxically, 78% of FMCG and CPG brands had NOT established a serious quick commerce presence despite: Their entire product categories are a perfect fit for 10-30 minute delivery; Quick commerce is generating 3-4x higher purchase frequency than traditional e-commerce; Urban consumers are now expecting these brands on quick commerce platforms.

Adomantra's initial audit of 12 prospective clients revealed three critical failure points:

  • Listing Invisibility (The Dark Store Discovery Problem): Quick commerce algorithms favor high-speed SKUs (products selling >100 units/day) and inventory availability. New sellers got buried on Page 4-5 of search results, with CTR dropping from 8% to 0.3%.
  • Listing Optimization Ignorance: Brands copied and pasted traditional e-commerce listings, ignoring the 45-50 character limit for product titles, search behaviors, thumbnail image requirements, and pricing psychology.
  • Performance Marketing Blindness: Traditional campaigns lacked hyperlocal pincode tracking and real-time inventory coordination, driving ads to out-of-stock items.
detaiedParagraph: When 10on10 Foods first came to Adomantra, the brand was practically invisible on quick commerce platforms despite having a strong product portfolio and an established name in the FMCG space. Their listings were buried deep in search results, inventory was consistently running out across key pincodes, and competitor brands were dominating every relevant search term. Customers searching for everyday staples like atta and flour were finding other brands first — not because 10on10's products were inferior, but because the brand had never learned the rules of this new game. The core problem was that they had approached quick commerce the same way they approached traditional e-commerce — copying listings across, proposing an overwhelming number of SKUs, and hoping the algorithm would figure it out. That approach does not work on quick commerce, where the algorithm rewards speed, availability, and hyper-local relevance above everything else.

Adomantra's first move was to completely rethink the portfolio strategy. Rather than trying to compete across hundreds of products simultaneously, the team identified the small set of SKUs that had the highest potential to gain traction quickly — products that matched how urban consumers actually search and shop on these platforms. Quick commerce users do not browse the way they do on traditional e-commerce sites. They type one or two words, they want it fast, and they make decisions in seconds. Listings were rebuilt from scratch to match this behavior — titles rewritten within the tight character limits the platform imposes, images replaced with clean packaging shots that dark store delivery agents could identify instantly without confusion, and every pricing decision carefully tested across multiple store locations to find the exact points where conversions happened most efficiently. Nothing was assumed. Everything was tested.

On the supply side, the work was equally detailed. Adomantra coordinated directly with dark store managers to understand demand patterns at the pincode level — because what sells heavily in one part of a city may move slowly in another, and a brand that treats its entire city as one market will always have stock in the wrong places. Automated alerts were set up so that whenever inventory dropped to a critical threshold, the supply chain was notified immediately, preventing the stockouts that had been quietly killing the brand's algorithmic ranking. As availability improved, the algorithm responded — rankings climbed, more customers discovered the products, and the cycle reinforced itself. By the end of the engagement, the brand had gone from near-invisible search rankings to consistently appearing among the top results for its core categories, stockouts had become a rare exception rather than a weekly crisis, customers who ordered once were coming back in large numbers within the same month, and average order values had grown meaningfully as shoppers began adding more to their baskets with each visit.

comprehensiveSummary.title:
Overview of the
Quick Commerce Market
comprehensiveSummary.heading: Why Were Brands Losing Money?
comprehensiveSummary.paragraph:
By early 2026, India's quick commerce market had become impossible to ignore. Yet paradoxically, 78% of FMCG and CPG brands had not established a serious quick commerce presence, despite several clear indicators that the channel was becoming essential for growth.

  • Their entire product categories were a natural fit for 10–30 minute delivery.
  • Quick commerce was generating 3–4x higher purchase frequency than traditional e-commerce.
  • Urban consumers increasingly expected these brands to be available across quick commerce platforms.

Three Critical Growth Barriers

When Adomantra conducted an initial audit of 12 prospective clients, three recurring challenges emerged that were preventing brands from succeeding in quick commerce.

Listing Invisibility (The Dark Store Discovery Problem)

Quick commerce algorithms prioritize products that demonstrate strong sales velocity, popularity signals, and consistent inventory availability. SKUs selling more than 100 units per day naturally receive greater visibility, while products showing 'In Stock' status are favored over those with consistent availability.

As a result, new sellers often found themselves buried deep within search results.

  • Average ranking position: Page 4–5
  • CTR decline from 8% at Position #1 to just 0.3% beyond Position #5

Without visibility, even quality products struggled to generate meaningful sales.

Listing Optimization Ignorance

Many brands approached quick commerce as if it were simply another e-commerce marketplace. However, the platform dynamics are fundamentally different.

campaignAudit.title: Quick Commerce Audit
campaignAudit.subtitle: Analysis of Critical Failure Points & Solutions

categories.name: Listing Visibility (Dark Store Discovery)
categories.checked: Algorithm optimization favors high-speed SKUs (>100 units/day) and availability signals.
categories.crossed: Page 4-5 placement on search, resulting in CTR dropping to 0.3%.

categories.name: Listing Optimization Strategy
categories.checked: Adopting short titles (45-50 chars), 1-2 word keywords, and thumbnail-friendly product shots.
categories.crossed: Copying traditional e-commerce listings with irrelevant discoverability and poor conversion.

categories.name: Performance Marketing Coordination
categories.checked: Syncing campaigns with dark store stock levels and hyper-local pincode targeting.
categories.crossed: Wasted ad budget driving traffic to pincodes where product is out of stock.

clientOverview.title:
Strategic
Interventions & Brand Baseline Profiles
clientOverview.description: We deployed specific strategies across 10on10 Foods, Kaya, and Patanjali to validate the effectiveness of our Quick Commerce Operating System.

dataBox1.title: Three Core Execution Components
items: Marketplace Optimization 10on10 Foods: Curating priority SKU lists, implementing A/B tested pricing, and streamlining dark store search visibility.
Performance Marketing Kaya: Synchronizing real-time dark store stock feeds with ad campaign budgets and scaling hyper-local pincode bids.
Omnichannel Strategy Patanjali: Orchestrating e-commerce subscriptions with quick commerce trials to reduce customer churn.

items.label: Brand Visibility Position:
items.value: Average rank at Page 4-5 of search

challenges.title: Key Strategic Failures <r>Addressed</r>
challenges.description: Most FMCG brands struggled to grow due to three major quick commerce challenges:

cards.title: Listing Invisibility
cards.body: New products got buried under popular listings and out-of-stock items, dropping CTR to 0.3%.

cards.title: Traditional Copy-Paste Content
cards.body: Using long titles and lifestyle pictures rather than search-friendly descriptions and clear thumbnail packaging.

cards.title: Hyper-Local Inventory Silos
cards.body: Driving premium traffic to pincodes where stock was empty, resulting in wasted ad spend and lost customers.

strategy.title: The 360-Degree Brand Growth <r>Phases</r>
strategy.description: Our campaign journey for the three FMCG brands followed a structured progression:

timeline.title: 10on10 Foods Optimization Case
timeline.p: Reduced listed items to 6 key SKUs driving 65% of demand. Shortened titles and implemented pincode-specific inventory alerts. Resulted in monthly revenue growth from ₹12 lakhs to ₹58 lakhs (+383%) with average search ranks reaching 2-3.

timeline.title: Kaya Performance Marketing Case
timeline.p: Deployed real-time inventory-aware campaigns across premium cosmetics. Ramped down budgets when stock fell under 2,000 units. Generated ₹31 crores in quick commerce revenue with a 7.2x average ROAS.

timeline.title: Patanjali Omnichannel Orchestration Case
timeline.p: Unified demand forecasting for both channels. Guided frequent quick commerce buyers to monthly e-commerce subscriptions, achieving ₹323 crores in combined revenue (+102% growth) and adding ₹38 crores in profit.

results.title: Performance Metrics Across Cohort <r>(12-Month Impact</r>)
results.description: Our knowledge-based strategy redefined quick commerce performance over a 12-month period. This led to increased revenue growth, improved ROAS, enhanced customer loyalty, and improved inventory management:

quote.body: "Adomantra did not employ fixed project costs, but aligned their incentives with our outcome targets. By combining marketplace optimization, performance marketing, and supply chain coordination, they acted as a true growth partner."
quote.author: FMCG Brand Cohort Lead

summaryFooter.title: Strategic Takeaways for Modern D2C & CPG <r>Brands</r>
items: Stock the Right Inventory: Focus on high-velocity items and hyper-local search visibility over large lists.
Pincode-Level Bidding: Adjust performance budgets dynamically by geographic demand and real-time inventory signals.
Omnichannel Coordination: Break down channel silos to build customer loyalty, increasing customer lifetime value.
